---
title: "Four Hands: Historical notes"
description: "I wrote a meditation on studying the kuzushiji text of 牛屋雑談. These are the citations that support the historical facts in that essay."
author: "Bo Laurent"
date: 2026-06-20
categories: [japanese, writing, Idle Chatter at the Beef Shop]
margin-footnotes: true
---

I wrote a meditation on studying the kuzushiji text of 牛屋雑談: [Four Hands: Between us, a whole reader](/blog/posts/four_hands.html). These are the citations that support the historical facts in that essay.

*Aguranabe* went through numerous editions — from the original 
woodblock-printed fascicles through moveable type, pocket editions, 
and eventually electronic text.
^[Wetherall, William. "Kanagaki Robun's Aguranabe." *Yosha Bunko*, 
2008. <https://www.yoshabunko.com/nishikie/articles/Aguranabe.html>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]

[Learn about how books were reproduced using woodblocks.](https://uknowledge.uky.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1058&context=libraries_facpub)^[Hioki, Kazuko. "Characteristics of Japanese Block Printed Books in the Edo Period: 1603–1867." *Library Faculty and Staff Publications* 58. University of Kentucky, 2009. <https://uknowledge.uky.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1058&context=libraries_facpub>. Accessed June 21, 2026.] 牛屋雑談 was published in chū bon size (about 5"x7").


Donald Keene translated parts of 牛屋雑談. 
^[Kanagaki Robun. "The Beefeater." Trans. Donald Keene. In 
*Modern Japanese Literature: An Anthology*. Ed. Donald Keene. 
New York: Grove Press, 1956, pp. 31–33.] I found a scan at <https://www1.udel.edu/History-old/figal/Hist370/text/er/beefeater.pdf>

Customers at early Meiji beef shops sat on tatami, not chairs.
^[Interior scene depicted in Kanagaki Robun, *Aguranabe*, vol. 1 
(Seishidō, 1871). Reproduced in Nippon.com, 
<https://www.nippon.com/en/features/c03911/>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]

Fukuzawa Yukichi's 1870 pamphlet *Nikushoku no setsu* urged Japanese readers 
to eat beef on grounds of health and national strength. 
^[Fukuzawa Yukichi. "On Eating Meat." 1870. Trans. Michael Bourdaghs. 
<https://www.bourdaghs.com/fukuzawa.htm>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]

Fukuzawa Yukichi's 1870 pamphlet *Nikushoku no setsu* urged Japanese readers 
to eat beef on grounds of health and national strength. 
^[福沢諭吉。「肉食之説」1870年。青空文庫。
<https://www.aozora.gr.jp/cards/000296/files/47343_42900.html>. 
2026年6月21日閲覧。Trans. Michael Bourdaghs as "On Eating Meat." 
<https://www.bourdaghs.com/fukuzawa.htm>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]

In Japan, beef was not widely eaten before the Meiji period.^[Wikipedia contributors. 
"History of Meat Consumption in Japan." *Wikipedia*, April 2026. 
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_meat_consumption_in_Japan>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]

Before 1872, ice in Japan was imported from Boston, a journey of some 
six months. Domestic ice first came to market in 1872, when Nakagawa Kahei 
began shipping ice from Hokkaido to Yokohama.^[Sakuraco. "Kakigori: Unlocking 
the Secrets of the Shaved Ice Delight." *Sakuraco Blog*. 
<https://sakura.co/blog/kakigori-unlocking-the-secrets-of-the-shaved-ice-delight>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]

In January 1872, the emperor ate beef publicly, effectively ending 
a twelve-century imperial prohibition on consuming meat.^[Wikipedia 
contributors. "History of Meat Consumption in Japan." *Wikipedia*, 
April 2026. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_meat_consumption_in_Japan>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]

By 1877, Tokyo reportedly had over 550 beef shops.^[Nippon.com. 
"Human-Animal Ties: Japanese Takes in Both Life and Death." 
January 2026. <https://www.nippon.com/en/features/c03911/>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]

The rickshaw was invented in 1869 and had only just entered production 
when *Aguranabe* was published. It was possible only after the Meiji 
government lifted the Tokugawa-era ban on wheeled vehicles.^[Wikipedia 
contributors. "Rickshaw." *Wikipedia*, 2026. 
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rickshaw#Japan>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]

Very few Japanese today can read this book, though it was a hit 
only 155 years ago.^[Kuzushiji was removed from the Japanese 
elementary school curriculum in 1900. Lamb, Alex. "How Machine 
Learning Can Help Unlock the World of Ancient Japan." *The Gradient*, 
November 17, 2019. <https://thegradient.pub/machine-learning-ancient-japan/>. 
Accessed June 21, 2026. See also: Center for Open Data in the Humanities 
(CODH). "KuroNet Kuzushiji Recognition Service." 
<https://codh.rois.ac.jp/kuronet/>. Accessed June 21, 2026.]
